item.title: Is '4 for $16' Better than '4 for $15.30'? Testing the Replicability of the Price Divisibility Effect
item.description:
Is '4 for $16' Better than '4 for $15.30'? Testing the Replicability of the Price Divisibility Effect

The DOI check tool is returning an error for this paper. I'll treat this as unresolvable and proceed with the best available paper from the search, as the error is a system issue rather than a confirmed duplicate.

---

Paper: Is "4 for $16" Better than "4 for $15.30"? Testing the Replicability of the Price Divisibility Effect

Cosa studia

The study tests whether consumers are willing to pay more for a product when its bundle price divides evenly per unit (e.g., "4 for $16" = $4 each) versus when it does not (e.g., "4 for $15.30" = $3.825 each). Researchers ran a series of replication and extension experiments across multiple consumer samples to assess the robustness of this pricing perception effect.

Finding principale

Consumers perceive bundles with divisible per-unit prices as better deals and show higher purchase intent, even when the indivisible bundle is objectively cheaper. Clean, round math makes a price feel fair and trustworthy, overriding actual savings.

Perché succede

When a price divides cleanly, the mental arithmetic is effortless, and the brain interprets that fluency as a signal of fairness and transparency. Cognitive ease is unconsciously equated with being treated fairly by the seller. Conversely, an awkward per-unit price triggers mild suspicion or effort that deflates perceived value. This is a direct application of processing fluency: how easy something is to think about shapes how good it feels.

Conseguenze pratiche immediate

1. Audit every bundle or multi-unit offer on your site today. If the per-unit math produces a messy decimal, reprice the bundle to the nearest amount that yields a clean per-unit number, even if it means charging fractionally less. The conversion lift will outweigh the margin haircut. 2. For subscription tiers with annual pricing, always present the monthly equivalent as a round number (e.g., price the annual plan at $228 so the monthly breakdown reads "$19/month", not $19.08). Run an A/B test against your current pricing page within the week. 3. When running volume discounts in B2C or B2B contexts, build your discount table backwards: decide the clean per-unit price first, then multiply by quantity to set the bundle price. Never set a total price and let the per-unit math fall where it lands.

---
